vulnerabilities.title: VoteWeighting.removeNominee Corrupts Global pointsSum Slope, Distorting All Emission Distributions
vulnerabilities.summary: When owner calls removeNominee(), the function zeros pointsWeight[nomineeHash][nextTime].bias and recalculates pointsSum[nextTime].bias as oldSum - oldWeight. However, it does NOT remove the nominee's slope contribution from pointsSum[nextTime].slope. The removed nominee's slope remains in the global sum, causing pointsSum.bias to decay faster than the actual sum of active nominee weights. This progressively distorts all nomineeRelativeWeight calculations and disrupts the entire staking incentiv

description.desc: pointsSum[nextTime].bias is updated to newSum but pointsSum[nextTime].slope is NOT adjusted to remove the removed nominee's slope contribution
Affected function: calculateStakingIncentives(uint256,uint256,bytes32,uint256)

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL: Every removal of a nominee with active votes permanently corrupts the global weight sum. The corruption compounds with each removal and grows over time as the excess slope drains the bias. This affects all staking incentive distributions across all chains, as Dispenser.calculateStakingIncentives relies on nomineeRelativeWeight. Staking targets may receive more or less OLAS than intended, with the error growing each epoch. Recovery requires contract upgrade or redeployment of VoteWeighting.

vulnerabilities.title: L2 Target Dispenser Batch Processing DoS via Malicious Staking Contract deposit() Revert
vulnerabilities.summary: In DefaultTargetDispenserL2._processData(), the verification of staking targets uses a low-level call (which handles failures gracefully), but the subsequent IStaking(target).deposit(amount) is a regular Solidity call. If a valid-looking staking target's deposit() function reverts, the entire _processData transaction reverts, blocking ALL other targets in the same bridge message batch from receiving their OLAS incentives.

description.desc: Low-level call for verification (line 147-148) vs regular call for deposit (line 165); deposit revert propagates to entire function
Affected function: createStakingInstance(address,bytes)

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH impact: All staking targets on the affected L2 chain are blocked from receiving OLAS incentives until the DAO intervenes via processDataMaintenance(). The bridge message tokens remain on the L2 dispenser but are not distributed. Recovery requires DAO governance action to call processDataMaintenance with data excluding the problematic target, which introduces delays (governance voting period). During this time, all staking programs on the affected chain are denied their incentives.

vulnerabilities.title: DefaultTargetDispenserL2.redeem Bypasses Staking Target Re-verification
vulnerabilities.summary: The redeem() function in DefaultTargetDispenserL2 allows anyone to process queued staking deposits by providing the target, amount, and batchNonce that match a stored queue hash. However, redeem() does NOT re-verify the staking target against StakingFactory. A target that was valid when queued but later disabled (via setInstanceStatus) or exceeded its emissions limit can still receive the full queued OLAS amount.

description.desc: redeem() checks only stakingQueueingNonces[queueHash] existence; no call to stakingFactory.verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The amount deposited was already capped at queue time, so the immediate financial impact is bounded by the amount that was legitimately allocated. However: (1) OLAS deposited to a disabled/invalid target may be misallocated or effectively lost if the target is malicious or non-functional; (2) The withheldAmount accounting is not updated since the amount was already approved at queue time; (3) This breaks the protocol invariant INV-STATE-019 that all deposits should be verified against the staking factory.

vulnerabilities.title: Tokenomics.refundFromStaking Uses Wrong Address in Error Message (depository instead of dispenser)
vulnerabilities.summary: In Tokenomics.refundFromStaking(), the access control check correctly validates msg.sender against the dispenser address, but the error message erroneously references the depository address: `revert ManagerOnly(msg.sender, depository)`. This is a definite code defect where the error reports the wrong required address.

description.desc: Access check is `dispenser != msg.sender` but error is `revert ManagerOnly(msg.sender, depository)` — should be dispenser

vulnerabilities.impact: LOW severity code defect. No financial impact as the access control logic itself is correct. The defect affects error reporting only: (1) Developers debugging failed transactions will see the wrong required address. (2) Monitoring tools parsing revert reasons will log incorrect data. (3) Front-end applications displaying error messages will show misleading information. The access control is NOT bypassed — security is maintained.

vulnerabilities.title: EthereumDepositProcessor._deposit Hard Reverts on Any Invalid Target, Blocking Entire Batch
vulnerabilities.summary: In EthereumDepositProcessor._deposit(), if any single target in a batch returns zero from verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount, the function reverts with TargetEmissionsZero, blocking ALL targets in the batch. This differs from L2 dispensers which gracefully withhold invalid amounts and continue processing. On L1, a single disabled staking instance blocks the entire chain's incentive distribution.

description.desc: if (limitAmount == 0) { revert TargetEmissionsZero(target); } — hard revert blocks entire batch
Affected function: setInstanceStatus(address,bool)

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH - A single malicious or negligent deployer can block the entire L1 batch incentive distribution by disabling their staking instance. All valid L1 staking targets in the same batch transaction are denied their incentives. While individual claims per-target would work for valid targets, batch efficiency is lost and the invalid target's individual claim also reverts. The inconsistency between L1 (hard revert) and L2 (graceful withholding) is a genuine code defect regardless of exploitation difficulty.

vulnerabilities.title: VoteWeighting Removed Nominee's changesSum Entries Persist and Distort Future Slope Adjustments
vulnerabilities.summary: When removeNominee is called, it does not clean up the changesWeight and changesSum entries scheduled at future lock expiration times of users who voted for the removed nominee. If users do not voluntarily call revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower, these scheduled slope decrements will fire at the original lock expiration times, incorrectly reducing the global sum's slope and further distorting weight calculations.

description.desc: removeNominee does not iterate over voters to clean changesSum/changesWeight at their lock expiration times

vulnerabilities.impact: The impact is progressive distortion of the global weight sum used to calculate all nominee relative weights. Since pointsSum.bias directly determines the denominator in the relative weight formula (weight = 1e18 * nomineeWeight / totalSum), an artificially low totalSum inflates all remaining nominees' relative weights. This affects every call to Dispenser.calculateStakingIncentives, which determines OLAS emission distribution across all staking targets. Over time, as more nominees are removed without full voter revocation, the distortion compounds. The severity depends on the proportion of voting power directed to removed nominees vs. total voting power.

vulnerabilities.title: Dispenser.claimOwnerIncentives Reverts When Reward and TopUp Are Both Zero
vulnerabilities.summary: In claimOwnerIncentives(), when both reward and topUp are zero, the code path skips the Treasury withdrawal (since reward + topUp == 0), leaving the `success` variable as its default value `false`. The function then hits `if (!success) { revert ClaimIncentivesFailed(...); }`, causing an uninformative revert instead of a graceful return with zero values.

description.desc: bool success; (default false) set inside conditional block; reverts when success is false even for legitimate zero-incentive case

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium severity code defect. No direct financial loss, but it breaks the expected interface contract: callers cannot distinguish 'zero incentives' from 'claim system failure'. Integration contracts using try-catch on claimOwnerIncentives will incorrectly classify zero-incentive cases as failures. Users must first call getOwnerIncentives (a view function) to check before claiming, adding friction and gas costs. The Tokenomics.accountOwnerIncentives function still performs state-modifying operations (zeroing balances, updating lastEpoch) before returning, but since the entire transaction reverts, these changes are harmlessly rolled back.

vulnerabilities.title: StakingFactory Owner Cannot Disable Malicious Staking Instances Created by Untrusted Deployers
vulnerabilities.summary: StakingFactory.setInstanceStatus() restricts instance enable/disable to the original deployer (msg.sender == instanceParams.deployer). The factory owner has NO override capability. If a deployer creates a staking instance that passes initial verification but later behaves maliciously, the factory owner cannot disable it. Since createStakingInstance is permissionless, any attacker can deploy instances that only they can control.

description.desc: if (msg.sender != deployer) revert OwnerOnly — only deployer can toggle; factory owner has no authority

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The factory owner lacks a direct emergency mechanism to disable specific malicious instances. While indirect mitigations exist (changing the verifier, removing the nominee from VoteWeighting), they require additional governance actions, contract deployments, and time. During this delay window, the malicious instance continues to be verified and can receive OLAS deposits from L2 dispensers. The impact is partially mitigated because: (1) VoteWeighting.removeNominee() can stop future emission allocation, (2) the verifier can be updated to reject the instance indirectly, and (3) the Dispenser can be paused in emergencies.

vulnerabilities.title: Donation Amount Truncation in _trackServiceDonations Creates Undistributed ETH
vulnerabilities.summary: In _trackServiceDonations(), each donation amount is divided by the number of service units: `uint96 amount = uint96(amounts[i] / numServiceUnits)`. The remainder is lost — it is counted in totalDonationsETH but never distributed to any unit's pendingRelativeReward or pendingRelativeTopUp. Over many donations with remainders, this creates a permanent discrepancy between recorded donations and actual distributed rewards.

description.desc: uint96 amount = uint96(amounts[i] / numServiceUnits); — integer division truncation, remainder lost

vulnerabilities.impact: Low financial impact per individual donation (at most numServiceUnits-1 wei lost per service per donation), but the defect is real and systematic. Over the protocol lifetime with many donations, the cumulative loss grows without bound. The undistributed ETH remains in the treasury, so it is not lost to the protocol but is lost to the intended unit owner recipients. Services with many units are disproportionately affected.

vulnerabilities.title: Permissionless Tokenomics initialization enables permanent takeover if deployed/used as standalone implementation
vulnerabilities.summary: Tokenomics.initializeTokenomics() is external and only guarded by `owner == address(0)`. If the Tokenomics implementation contract is deployed and left uninitialized (or is intended to be used standalone), any attacker can initialize it first, becoming `owner` and permanently controlling managers (treasury/depository/dispenser), registries, parameters, and the PROXY_TOKENOMICS implementation slot used as a sentinel for checkpoint().

description.desc: external initializer without access control beyond 'only once' check

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL. If exploited on the proxy, the attacker gains complete control over the tokenomics system: they can redirect treasury rewards, change inflation parameters, modify incentive fractions, set arbitrary registries, and change the tokenomics implementation. This would allow manipulation of all OLAS emission flows, staking incentives, and bond programs. Even if exploited only on the implementation contract (not proxy), it represents a code-level defect that violates secure initialization best practices.

vulnerabilities.title: Wormhole Bridge Precision Loss Causes Permanent L2 Staking Queue Lockup
vulnerabilities.summary: Wormhole's TokenBridge truncates ERC20 transfers to 8 decimals. When Dispenser processes a claim with a withheldAmount deduction, the transferAmount can have up to 18 decimals of precision. The WormholeDepositProcessorL1 sends this amount via Wormhole, which silently truncates the dust. However, the cross-chain message payload encodes the ORIGINAL stakingIncentives array. On L2, WormholeTargetDispenserL2 attempts to distribute the full stakingIncentives amount, but its actual OLAS balance is sho

description.desc: sendTokenWithPayloadToEvm(..., transferAmount, ...) truncates to 8 decimals while payload contains 18-decimal amounts
Affected function: receivePayloadAndTokens

vulnerabilities.impact: LOW-MEDIUM severity, not CRITICAL as claimed. The code defect is real: syncWithheldAmount lacks normalization that syncWithheldAmountMaintenance includes, creating a discrepancy in Wormhole token transfers. Each affected batch loses at most 1e10-1 wei (~10^-8 OLAS), which could cause the last staking target to be queued instead of directly deposited. However: (1) queued items are redeemable via redeem(), (2) subsequent batches bring enough tokens to cover previous deficits, (3) the DAO has processDataMaintenance for recovery, (4) the amount at risk per incident is negligible.

vulnerabilities.title: Array Out-of-Bounds DoS in unstake Due to Stale Index After Eviction
vulnerabilities.summary: In StakingBase.sol, unstake calls checkpoint(), which may evict inactive services. The _evict function removes services by swapping them with the last element in setServiceIds and popping. However, unstake determines the index (idx) of the unstaking service from a COPY of the array taken BEFORE evictions occur. If another service is evicted during the checkpoint, and the unstaking service happens to be at the end of the array, _evict will move the unstaking service and reduce the array length. W

description.desc: setServiceIds[idx] = setServiceIds[setServiceIds.length - 1]; uses stale idx from before checkpoint()

vulnerabilities.impact: The impact is a conditional DoS on unstake(). When checkpoint triggers evictions, users whose services are at higher indices than evicted services cannot unstake in the same transaction. The workaround is to call checkpoint() separately first. This makes the bug a medium-severity DoS rather than a permanent fund lock. However, it violates the expected atomicity of unstake() and can cause confusing reverts for users. If multiple inactive services exist, the condition could repeatedly occur.

vulnerabilities.title: Dispenser.retain() Callable During Staking Incentive Pause, Advancing Retainer Epoch Counter and Modifying Staking Inflation
vulnerabilities.summary: The retain() function in Dispenser.sol lacks any pause state check, unlike claimStakingIncentives and claimStakingIncentivesBatch which both verify paused state and treasury paused state. This allows anyone to call retain() during a StakingIncentivesPaused or AllPaused state, advancing mapLastClaimedStakingEpochs[retainerHash] and calling ITokenomics.refundFromStaking() which modifies the current epoch's staking incentive accounting. During a security pause meant to freeze all staking-related op

description.desc: retain() has reentrancy guard but NO pause check; contrast with claimStakingIncentives lines 490-493 which check paused == StakingIncentivesPaused || paused == AllPaused || ITreasury(treasury).paused() == 2

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium severity. The pause mechanism for staking incentive accounting is incomplete. During an emergency pause meant to freeze all staking-related operations, retain() can still: (1) advance the retainer's claimed epoch counter, which is irreversible; (2) modify staking inflation state via refundFromStaking(), adding amounts back to the staking incentive pool for the current epoch. While the economic impact is limited to inflation pool accounting adjustments (returning unused incentives), it directly violates the intended invariant that all staking incentive state modifications are halted during a pause.

vulnerabilities.title: Tokenomics.changeTokenomicsParameters Uses Unsafe uint72/uint32 Casts Enabling Silent Parameter Truncation
vulnerabilities.summary: In changeTokenomicsParameters(), input parameters are cast to smaller types (uint72 for devsPerCapital/codePerDev, uint32 for epochLen) BEFORE being compared against bounds. For example, 'if (uint72(_devsPerCapital) > MIN_PARAM_VALUE)' truncates a uint256 to uint72 before comparison. If the owner (likely a DAO governance contract) passes a value like 2^72 + 1e15, the uint72 cast truncates to 1e15 which passes the MIN_PARAM_VALUE check, and the parameter is set to 1e15 instead of the intended lar

description.desc: if (uint72(_devsPerCapital) > MIN_PARAM_VALUE) { devsPerCapital = uint72(_devsPerCapital); } — cast before comparison allows values > 2^72 to truncate and pass the check

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium severity. The defect allows silent parameter truncation in an owner-only function. While the owner is expected to be a governance contract, governance proposals specify uint256 values and rely on the target function to validate them properly. Silent truncation means: (1) devsPerCapital/codePerDev affect the IDF calculation, potentially distorting tokenomics incentives; (2) epochLen affects epoch timing, which impacts inflation distribution; (3) veOLASThreshold affects top-up eligibility for service owners. All truncated values take effect at the next epoch checkpoint and require another governance cycle to correct.

vulnerabilities.title: Native ETH Permanently Locked if `migrate` is Called Before `drain`
vulnerabilities.summary: The `migrate` function in L2 target dispensers transfers all OLAS tokens and permanently locks the contract by zeroing the owner. If the contract holds native ETH, it becomes permanently trapped because `drain` can no longer be called.

description.desc: Irreversible privilege revocation without sweeping all asset types: `owner = address(0)` leaves `address(this).balance` stranded.

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The amount of native ETH at risk depends on bridge refund patterns and operational usage. On chains where bridge operations frequently return native token refunds, the locked amount could be non-trivial. The loss is permanent and irrecoverable. The impact is limited because: (1) the amount of native ETH is typically small relative to OLAS holdings, (2) it requires the specific operational sequence of migration without prior drain, (3) it only affects native ETH, not OLAS tokens which are the primary asset.

vulnerabilities.title: `VoteWeighting` Checkpoint Stagnation if Missed for Over 53 Weeks
vulnerabilities.summary: If a nominee or the global sum is not checkpointed for more than 53 weeks, the state advancement loop completes without updating the timestamp, causing an infinite loop of stagnation where the state can never catch up to the present.

description.desc: Loop bound prevents state progression: `if (t > block.timestamp) { timeSum = t; }` is unreachable if elapsed time > `MAX_NUM_WEEKS`.

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. Individual nominees can become permanently non-functional if not checkpointed for 54+ weeks. The global sum stagnation (while unlikely) would permanently halt the entire staking incentive distribution system. Recovery requires contract upgrade/redeployment. The code defect is clear: the time pointer should be updated unconditionally after the loop.

vulnerabilities.title: Tokenomics.trackServiceDonations() does not validate `amounts.length == serviceIds.length`, enabling treasury-originated misaccounting or unexpected reverts inside _trackServiceDonations
vulnerabilities.summary: trackServiceDonations() iterates over serviceIds to validate existence, but never checks amounts array length matches serviceIds length before passing both into _trackServiceDonations(). Inside _trackServiceDonations(), it indexes amounts[i]. If amounts is shorter, the call reverts; if longer, extra amounts are ignored. While only treasury can call trackServiceDonations(), a bug/misconfiguration or compromised treasury could cause donation accounting DoS or partial incentive allocation inconsist

description.desc: missing array length equality check across correlated arrays

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The defect is a missing input validation (inconsistent with other functions in the same contract). Impact depends on treasury behavior: DoS (shorter amounts → full revert) or accounting inconsistency (longer amounts → silent under-tracking). Since the caller must be the trusted treasury, exploitation requires a treasury bug or compromise. However, the code itself is objectively defective per the codebase's own standards.

vulnerabilities.title: StakingToken.deposit() updates accounting before transferFrom, enabling balance/availableRewards inflation if stakingToken is non-standard (reentrant or returns true but transfers less)
vulnerabilities.summary: StakingToken.deposit() increments `balance` and `availableRewards` before calling SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom(). If the stakingToken is a non-standard ERC20 that can re-enter (e.g., ERC777 hooks or malicious token) or otherwise cause the transfer to behave unexpectedly (e.g., transfer less than amount but still returns success through crafted return data), internal accounting may temporarily or permanently diverge from actual token balances, potentially violating staking solvency invariants

description.desc: effects-before-interactions; ERC20 transferFrom cannot be assumed to transfer exact amount

vulnerabilities.impact: If exploited with fee-on-transfer tokens: progressive insolvency where the contract's internal balance exceeds actual token holdings. Late unstakers/claimers will be unable to withdraw their rewards, causing permanent loss. With reentrant tokens: potential to manipulate reward allocation in checkpoint() during the callback, extracting rewards not backed by actual deposits. Severity is HIGH when non-standard tokens are used, but LOW if only OLAS (standard ERC20) is deployed.

vulnerabilities.title: StakingNativeToken._withdraw() can be weaponized for DoS by multisig recipients that intentionally revert on receiving ETH
vulnerabilities.summary: StakingNativeToken._withdraw() uses `to.call{value: amount}("")` and reverts if the call fails. If the service multisig address is a contract that rejects ETH (reverts/fallback consumes too much gas), then any claim/unstake that tries to pay rewards will revert, potentially preventing service owners from unstaking (if reward > 0) and blocking reward claims indefinitely.

description.desc: hard-revert on native transfer failure to recipient contract
Affected function: unstake

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium-High impact. Affected services lose access to their service NFT and accumulated rewards. The locked service also occupies a staking slot (counted toward maxNumServices), reducing capacity for other services. While the attacker doesn't directly profit from others' funds, they can grief the staking pool by permanently occupying slots. Recovery requires either: (a) the multisig implementation being changed back to accept ETH (if possible), or (b) protocol-level migration/emergency intervention (no such mechanism exists in the current code).

vulnerabilities.title: Wormhole Bridge Decimal Normalization Mismatch Causes Permanent Dust Loss
vulnerabilities.summary: Withheld amounts synced from L2 to L1 are not normalized on L1. When deducted from a normalized `transferAmount` during `claimStakingIncentives`, the resulting amount becomes un-normalized. When sent via the Wormhole bridge (which truncates to 8 decimals), the dust is permanently stuck in the `WormholeDepositProcessorL1` contract.

description.desc: transferAmount -= withheldAmount where withheldAmount is not normalized

vulnerabilities.impact: LOW to MEDIUM severity. Per-transaction dust loss is at most ~10 gwei of OLAS. Over thousands of transactions, cumulative loss grows but remains economically small. The primary concern is the code defect itself — an inconsistency between syncWithheldAmount (no normalization) and syncWithheldAmountMaintenance (normalizes), indicating the developers were aware of the issue but missed the automated path. The locked tokens in WormholeDepositProcessorL1 are irrecoverable since owner is address(0) and there's no withdrawal function.

vulnerabilities.title: VoteWeighting changesSum Underflow DoS After Nominee Removal
vulnerabilities.summary: After removeNominee zeroes a nominee's bias but does NOT clean up future scheduled slope changes in changesSum/changesWeight, the _getSum() internal function can underflow when processing those residual slope decrements. Since _getSum() is called by voteForNomineeWeights, checkpointNominee, and nomineeRelativeWeightWrite, the entire VoteWeighting system (and thus staking incentive distribution) can be permanently DoS'd.

description.desc: _getSum() performs pt.slope -= dSlope without underflow protection; removeNominee zeros bias but not slope, leaving residual changesSum entries from unrevoked votes

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL. Once triggered, _getSum() permanently reverts, which blocks: (1) voteForNomineeWeights - no more voting, (2) checkpoint/checkpointNominee - no weight checkpointing, (3) nomineeRelativeWeightWrite - no weight calculations, (4) Dispenser.calculateStakingIncentives (calls checkpointNominee) - no staking incentive distribution. The entire protocol's staking incentive distribution is permanently halted until the VoteWeighting contract is replaced.

vulnerabilities.title: Dispenser retain() Uses Stale Retainer Weight Due to Missing Checkpoint
vulnerabilities.summary: The retain() function in Dispenser queries nomineeRelativeWeight for the retainer without first calling checkpointNominee. Unlike calculateStakingIncentives which explicitly checkpoints, retain() reads potentially stale weight data. This causes incorrect return amounts, leaving OLAS in the staking inflation pool instead of being properly returned.

description.desc: retain() calls nomineeRelativeWeight (view) without prior checkpointNominee; calculateStakingIncentives at line 584 calls checkpointNominee before weight queries
Affected function: _nomineeRelativeWeight(bytes32,uint256,uint256)

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. When retain() reads stale weight data, the retainer's proportional share of staking incentives is not returned to the inflation pool. The epochs are marked as claimed (mapLastClaimedStakingEpochs is updated) so this lost amount cannot be recovered. Over time, this leads to a permanent leakage of OLAS from the staking inflation system. The severity depends on how much voting weight the retainer has and how many epochs pass without the retainer being checkpointed. Anyone can mitigate this by calling checkpointNominee for the retainer before calling retain(), but the function itself is defective.

vulnerabilities.title: StakingFactory verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount Trusts Attacker-Controlled emissionsAmount
vulnerabilities.summary: The verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount function reads emissionsAmount from the staking instance itself (IStaking(instance).emissionsAmount()). Since staking instances are created permissionlessly via the factory, a malicious implementation could return an inflated emissionsAmount value, allowing the staking target to receive more OLAS than its actual capacity warrants.

description.desc: amount = IStaking(instance).emissionsAmount() reads from potentially attacker-controlled proxy; limitAmount check only applies verifier's global max, not actual instance capacity

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The defect allows staking targets to receive up to the global emissionsLimit regardless of their actual capacity. With whitelisted implementations, excess OLAS stays locked in the staking contract (no theft, but capital inefficiency and potential disruption of emission distribution). With whitelisting disabled, the impact could be HIGH as malicious implementations could extract deposited funds. The global emissionsLimit cap prevents unbounded extraction.

vulnerabilities.title: StakingVerifier verifyInstance Allows Non-OLAS Staking Contracts When stakingToken Reverts
vulnerabilities.summary: StakingVerifier.verifyInstance performs an optional check for the staking token. If the stakingToken() call reverts (success=false), the check is skipped entirely. A malicious staking contract could implement stakingToken() to revert, effectively hiding that it uses a non-OLAS token while passing all other verification checks.

description.desc: staticcall to stakingToken(); if success==false, check is skipped entirely; malicious contract can revert to bypass OLAS token requirement

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium severity. The defect allows OLAS emission funds directed to a verified staking target to be deposited into a malicious contract that can extract them. The impact is bounded by: (a) the voting weight directed to the malicious target, (b) the emissions amount limits enforced by the verifier, and (c) the requirement that implementationsCheck is false or the attacker's implementation is somehow whitelisted. The defect exists in the verification logic regardless of exploitability.

vulnerabilities.title: Tokenomics._finalizeIncentivesForUnitId Silent uint96 Truncation on Reward and TopUp Accumulation
vulnerabilities.summary: In _finalizeIncentivesForUnitId(), the accumulated reward and topUp values are computed in uint256 but cast to uint96 without overflow checks: `mapUnitIncentives[unitType][unitId].reward = uint96(totalIncentives)` and `mapUnitIncentives[unitType][unitId].topUp = uint96(totalIncentives)`. If totalIncentives exceeds type(uint96).max (~7.9e28), the value silently truncates, permanently destroying accumulated rewards.

description.desc: uint96(totalIncentives) cast without overflow check; totalIncentives computed from multi-factor multiplication

vulnerabilities.impact: Theoretical: permanent, irrecoverable loss of accumulated rewards/topUps for affected component/agent owners due to silent truncation. Practical: negligible — the economic parameters of the protocol (ETH total supply, OLAS inflation schedule) make the overflow condition unreachable within centuries. The contract comments explicitly justify uint96 with analyses showing 130+ year (ETH) and 220+ year (OLAS) safety margins, which effectively cover this case despite not explicitly addressing the intermediate sum.

vulnerabilities.title: Dispenser claimStakingIncentives can transfer/mint OLAS to a zero or malicious depositProcessor if chainId mapping is unset or set to non-contract
vulnerabilities.summary: claimStakingIncentives / claimStakingIncentivesBatch use mapChainIdDepositProcessors[chainId] without validating it is non-zero and a contract before calling getBridgingDecimals() and transferring OLAS to it. If the mapping is unset (0) or accidentally set to an EOA, calls will revert (DoS) or OLAS may be transferred to an address that cannot forward it across the bridge (funds stuck).

description.desc: unvalidated external call to potentially-zero / non-contract address

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM impact. The defect causes DoS for claims on affected chainIds, but (1) requires owner/admin action to trigger (setting processor to zero), (2) is recoverable by setting the processor back to a valid contract, (3) does NOT cause fund loss since reverts are atomic, (4) does NOT affect claims for other chainIds. The most significant impact is that syncWithheldAmountMaintenance is also blocked, creating an operational gap during disabled periods. The staking incentives accumulate but are not lost.

vulnerabilities.title: StakingBase Checkpoint Reward Rounding Systematically Favors First Eligible Service in Array
vulnerabilities.summary: When totalRewards exceeds lastAvailableRewards in the checkpoint function, rewards are proportionally scaled down. The loop processes services from index 1 first, then handles service[0] last, assigning any rounding dust (lastAvailableRewards - updatedTotalRewards) to it. The first eligible service consistently receives extra wei from rounding. While individually tiny, this is a systematic code defect in reward distribution fairness.

description.desc: Loop starts from i=1, processes service[0] last with dust addition: if (lastAvailableRewards > updatedTotalRewards) { updatedReward += lastAvailableRewards - updatedTotalRewards; }

vulnerabilities.impact: Negligible. Maximum impact is (maxNumServices - 1) wei per checkpoint, typically 1-9 wei. Over 1000 checkpoints with 10 services, the total bias is at most 9000 wei (9e-15 ETH/OLAS). This does not constitute a meaningful economic impact and is a standard approach to integer rounding in Solidity.

vulnerabilities.title: Unrestricted nominee creation enables attacker-controlled staking target to siphon cross-chain staking emissions
vulnerabilities.summary: Because VoteWeighting allows anyone to add nominees, an attacker can list their own staking target (on some chainId) as a nominee and then use legitimate veOLAS voting (including their own veOLAS, or apathetic voter participation) to route a meaningful fraction of staking incentives toward a malicious staking contract that can immediately extract/redirect deposited OLAS.

description.desc: addNomineeNonEVM is external with no OwnerOnly; voteForNomineeWeights updates gauge weights permissionlessly

vulnerabilities.impact: The permissionless nominee addition is a design choice consistent with gauge systems. The downstream verification layer (StakingFactory + StakingVerifier) provides adequate defense when properly configured. The withheld amount mechanism handles invalid targets correctly. The staking contract mechanics (activity checks, minimum duration, bounded rewards) constrain value extraction even for valid targets. This is not a code defect but a system design that requires proper configuration of the verification layer.

vulnerabilities.title: Unprivileged caller can add arbitrary nominees to VoteWeighting, forcing Dispenser.addNominee() and potentially freezing staking incentive claiming via mass nominee spam
vulnerabilities.summary: VoteWeighting allows permissionless nominee creation (addNomineeEVM/addNomineeNonEVM). When a Dispenser is configured, VoteWeighting auto-calls Dispenser.addNominee(nomineeHash), which writes mapLastClaimedStakingEpochs[nomineeHash] = epochCounter. A spammer can create a very large nominee set, increasing operational overhead and potentially making routine checkpointing / claiming workflows impractical (off-chain processes that enumerate nominees, governance dashboards, or operational scripts).

description.desc: addNomineeNonEVM / addNomineeEVM are external with no owner check; _addNominee() calls IDispenser(dispenser).addNominee(nomineeHash) when dispenser != 0
Affected function: addNominee(bytes32)

vulnerabilities.impact: The confirmed defect has the following real impacts: (1) Permanent storage bloat in both VoteWeighting and Dispenser contracts; (2) getAllNominees() view function becomes expensive/unusable with large nominee sets; (3) Off-chain tools/dashboards enumerating nominees face degraded performance; (4) Owner faces significant operational burden to remove spam nominees (one-by-one, timing constrained); (5) Each spam nominee gets initialized claim state in Dispenser. The on-chain staking incentive claiming process is NOT affected as it operates per-nominee without iterating over all nominees.
